title: Tuning quantum tunneling in WSe$_2$ via strain engineering
authors: Rachid El Aitouni, Hasna Chnafa, Clarence Cortes, David Laroze, Ahmed Jellal
abstract: We present a comprehensive theoretical study of strain-engineered quantum transport in monolayer tungsten diselenide (WSe$_2$) in the presence of an electrostatic scalar potential. By incorporating strain effects within a low-energy Dirac framework, we analyze their impact on spin- and valley-resolved transmission, conductance, and polarization. The applied potential barrier partitions the system into three distinct regions, allowing for an analytical derivation of the wave functions in each domain. Enforcing continuity conditions at the interfaces yields exact expressions for the transmission and reflection amplitudes. The transmission probability is evaluated from the corresponding current densities, while the conductance is obtained using the Landauer-Büttiker formalism, enabling a quantitative determination of spin and valley polarizations. Our numerical analysis reveals that strain acts as a powerful tuning parameter that reshapes the electronic dispersion and strongly modifies transport characteristics. In particular, the transmission and conductance exhibit pronounced oscillatory behavior driven by quantum interference and resonant tunneling mechanisms. More importantly, both spin and valley polarizations display substantial and highly controllable variations as functions of strain, barrier height, and incident energy. These results demonstrate that strain and electrostatic engineering provide an efficient and versatile platform for manipulating spin-valley degrees of freedom in WSe$_2$. The ability to tailor polarization and interference effects suggests promising opportunities for the design of next-generation spintronic, valleytronic, and optoelectronic devices based on two-dimensional transition-metal dichalcogenides.
summary: This theoretical study demonstrates that mechanical strain can be used as a 'control knob' to manipulate the spin and valley degrees of freedom in WSe2. By combining strain with electrostatic engineering, researchers can create highly efficient, reconfigurable filters for quantum transport. This provides a promising pathway for designing next-generation valleytronic and spintronic devices.
main_problem: Investigating how uniaxial strain and electrostatic potentials can be used to tune quantum transport, specifically spin and valley polarizations, in monolayer WSe2.
main_result: Strain acts as a powerful tuning parameter that modifies electronic dispersion and induces oscillations in conductance and transmission, enabling highly controllable spin and valley filtering.
method: Analytical solution of the Dirac equation using a transfer matrix approach and the Landauer-Buttiker formalism to calculate conductance and polarization.
model_or_system: A monolayer of tungsten diselenide (WSe2) featuring a rectangular electrostatic potential barrier and subject to uniaxial strain, modeled via a low-energy Dirac framework with spin-orbit coupling and a strain-induced gauge field.
key_observables: Transmission probability, conductance, spin polarization, and valley polarization.
important_parameters: Strain (u_xx), barrier height (V0), barrier width (D), incident energy (E), and incident angle (phi).
main_assumptions: Assumes a low-energy Dirac-like band structure, ballistic transport, and uniaxial strain along the transport direction with uniform deformation.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Schematic of the strained WSe2 system; Fig 2: Transmission probability vs. incident angle; Fig 3: Spin-up transmission vs. strain; Fig 4: Spin-up/down transmission vs. barrier height; Fig 5: Conductance vs. strain; Fig 6: Polarization vs. barrier width; Fig 7: Spin and valley polarization vs. strain.
paper_structure: Introduction of the physical system and problem, description of the Dirac Hamiltonian and strain-induced gauge field, derivation of transmission/reflection coefficients via boundary conditions, numerical results for transport observables, and discussion of applications in spintronics/valleytronics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic representation of a WSe2 sheet subjected to a uniaxial strain along with an external scalar electrostatic potential of height V0 and width D in the central region.

FIG. 2. Transmission probability in the K valley as a function of the incident angle ϕ, for spin-up (solid line) and spin-down (dashed line). Three incident energies are considered: 0.9 eV (red, green), 1.1 eV (black, magenta), and 1.3 eV (blue, cyan), with ε = 1 and V0 = 3 eV.

Figure 2 displays the transmission probability in the K valley as a function of the incident angle ϕ for dif- ferent barrier widths D and incident energies E, un- der finite uniaxial strain. A prominent feature is the near-unity transmission at normal incidence (ϕ = 0) for all considered parameters, which constitutes a clear sig- nature of Klein tunneling in monolayer WSe2, similar to the behavior previously reported in graphene-based Dirac systems [15, 74]. FIG. 3. Spin-up transmission probability in the K valley as a function of strain ε for three different incident energy values; E = 1.1 eV (black dashed line), E = 1.3 eV (blue dashed line) and E = 1.5 eV (red line), with D = 1 nm and V0 = 3 eV.
FIG. 4. Spin-up transmission (black line) and spin-down transmission (red line) in the K valley as a function of the barrier height V0 for D = 5 nm, ε = 1 and E = 1.3 eV.
